content:
This is a fairly procrastinated start to my personal blog. Starting a blog isn’t as easy as it seems—I don’t want to waste people’s time with casual anecdotes. Meanwhile, an overly formal academic write-up would likely be overkill and scare people away.
There are many people who truly enjoy machine learning and find joy in sharing knowledge. I’ve been a long-time follower of AI/tech blogs from Andrej Karpathy, Lilian Weng, Yao Fu, and others. I usually prefer blogs over papers because blogs feel more honest and less AI-polished (or written to attract citations). Yet almost everyone I followed stopped posting in early 2025. I understand the shifts and hype in SF lately that keep everyone busy building and/or financially free. Still, I’d be sad if this vibe disappears—it’s been truly helpful to me over the past few years, along with many others.
I’ve been more of a builder than a writer, hesitant to publish conclusions without solid ground. But lately I’ve found it really fruitful to do deep dives into the state of things and the literature before engineering. A blog of “study notes” not only helps me organize thoughts, but also carries on that missing vibe of sharing knowledge in a blunt and unpretentious way. And so I finally made up my mind to start writing—and I truly hope you enjoy it. Stay tuned and keep the spark going!
What to Expect I’ll be writing about:
Tech Hacks: Most of the time, truth in tech world doesn’t reside in reports or posts but hidden in code. I enjoy digging these gems out to see the real flow. Different AI Research: I continuously keep track of frontier AI research and especially favor the non-incremental ones, like new architectures to model intelligence. Stories and Gossip: The true SOTA usually come from stories and gossip shared through friends, meetups, and conferences.

title: Why I start to write

content:
Binfeng Xu I’m a research engineer at NVIDIA. Currently, I work on agent harness and RL for computer-use and continual learning.
Formerly, I was a researcher at Samsung Research (SRA) where I led LLM post-training + distillation infra. I enjoy training large neural nets, building open-source projects and competing on Kaggle, where I rank top 1% globally.
Papers Gentopia: A Collaborative Platform for Tool-Augmented LLMs
Binfeng Xu, Xukun Liu, Hua Shen, Zeyu H, Yuhan L, Murong Y, Zhiyuan P, Yuchen L, Ziyu Y, Dongkuan Xu
ReWOO: Decoupling Reasoning from Observations for Efficient Augmented Language Models
Binfeng Xu, Zhiyuan Peng, Bowen Lei, Subhabrata Mukherjee, Yuchen Liu, Dongkuan Xu
Dynamic Noise Preference Optimization for LLM Self-Improvement
Haoyan Yang, Ting Hua, Shangqian Gao, Binfeng Xu, Zheng Tang, Jie Xu, Hongxia Jin, Vijay Srinivasan
Efficient Computation of Tucker Decomposition of Correlation-Based Tensors
Binfeng Xu, Grey Ballard, Robert Lyday, Paul Laurienti
Misc I play all games by Hidetaka Miyazaki, who motivated me once into indie Game Dev. Photography @Instagram; I enjoy Art. Minimalist.

Binfeng Xu

I’m a research engineer at NVIDIA. Currently, I work on agent harness and RL for computer-use and continual learning.

Formerly, I was a researcher at Samsung Research (SRA) where I led LLM post-training + distillation infra. I enjoy training large neural nets, building open-source projects and competing on Kaggle, where I rank top 1% globally.

Papers

  • Gentopia: A Collaborative Platform for Tool-Augmented LLMs
    Binfeng Xu, Xukun Liu, Hua Shen, Zeyu H, Yuhan L, Murong Y, Zhiyuan P, Yuchen L, Ziyu Y, Dongkuan Xu

content:
Modern AI agents are typically scaffolded with a runtime sandbox, and these Computer-Use Agents (CUA) autonomously run code, use the terminal, take notes, and access the Internet and MCPs – exactly like humans do when interacting with the digital world.
Yet the underlying reasoning and practices remain unclear to most, so let’s dive into popular agent scaffolds like Claude Code and MiniMax Agent, demystifying the design principles and discovering how agents benefit from using a computer.
Why Sandbox? In short, Context Delegation and Runtime Isolation.
Context Delegation. Even with memory-efficient tricks like KV caching and Linear Attention architectures, long-context reasoning is still a nontrivial challenge to AI agents. Production AI agents are usually packed with bloated tool descriptions and system prompts to cover more case handling and example following. Context length can easily grow to 100k or over 1M in multi-turn agentic trajectories – especially when tool responses contain extraneous data. Growing contexts dilute attention and cause huge memory burden to both training and inference.
Intuitively, you’d want to move episodic context out of the main agent loop using:
Subagent: Spin up subtasks to invoke another agent. Without sharing a full context, subagents can easily duplicate work, wasting excessive tokens, while over-engineered orchestration introduces inductive bias. Filesystem: Use the filesystem to keep todo lists, agent memory, and conditional instructions (aka Agent Skill) that the agent chooses to load into the context when necessary. Sandbox Filesystem is an agent’s extended context through computer-use. Runtime Isolation. Sandboxing keeps all agent actions in a tightly controlled environment, protecting the host system by isolating potential errors and de-risking real user data or resources. That isolation is especially critical when executing popular <python> and <browser> tools.
Cursor (Agent mode) running commands in a secure sandbox
Claude Code – More than just Code Claude Code has gained huge traction in 2025. It was originally built for coding assistance, but Anthropic is clearly steering it toward more general agent use cases. As highlighted in recent podcasts, Claude Code already excels at, or can be extended to, deep research and vertical specialist agents. Andrej has also been tweeting mini projects like agentic home control in the physical world.
Claude Code scaffolding. The agent controls a virtual sandbox with Bash and coding. Context delegated to filesystem (Skills). Tools executed outside the sandbox are implemented as MCP.
Agentic Computer Use What’s the magic here? Metaphorically, an AI Agent equipped with a Filesystem and Runtime Environment is just a human using a computer – the primary, if not only interface connecting humans to the Digital World. Imagine the action space. ¯_(ツ)_/¯ With that lens, scaffolding an agent turns into OS design:
Configuring a Linux Docker / VM, or even creating a completely native OS from scratch (yes, there are a few working on it). Designing the Filesystem within the sandbox. Pre-install some “Apps” for the agents – a Terminal, a Browser, a writing pad … and place some instructions for the agent to refer to before use (Anthropic names it SKILL.md). Meanwhile, give agents a “shortcut” to invoke outside endpoints – like credential-related database querying. These fit into the bucket of traditional function-calling or MCP. Claude Code System Prompt Anthropic still doesn’t publicly share Claude Code’s system prompt and tool implementations in its Claude Agent SDK. However, there’s an interesting thread where people trace and hack Claude Code’s request & response, and reverse engineer the system prompt and its commit diff across versions. From that prompt, we can clearly find some sparks and lessons in agent tool implementations.
For example, Claude Code doesn’t use an interactive browser like browser-use does by default. Instead it splits capabilities into separate WebSearch and WebFetch tools, likely due to speed & efficiency concerns.
Summary of Claude Code’s tools from the hacked system prompt.
Claude Code Filesystem When locally deployed, Claude Code uses ~/.claude/ for its own sandbox workspace, and it mounts your work project under ~/.claude/projects/. The scaffold keeps plugins (MCP integrations) and skills in different directories, and also tracks TODO, personalization configs, and metadata like command history and debug logs.
Claude Code’s working filesystem.
MiniMax Agent MiniMax has been the most surprising AI lab for me this year. It ships the #1 (as of Dec 2025) OSS model on LMArena WebDev at 229B weights, which is considered “small” among parallel flagship models. Meanwhile, the agent scaffold, MiniMax Agent creates surprisingly good apps and reports with prolonged reasoning and autonomous execution. Here’s my favorite trajectory replay.
MiniMax Agent using browser for autonomous app creation (Netflix Clone)
MiniMax Agent has a UI component that lets you navigate the agent’s sandbox filesystem in realtime, and you can prompt the agent to summarize its initial filesystem state. The workspace is a Python-based development environment designed for AI agents with integrated external API access capabilities. Rich modular data sources connect to various third-party APIs including Twitter, Yahoo Finance, TripAdvisor, Pinterest, Patents, Scholar, Commodities, Metals, and Booking.com. None of these live inside the system prompt, which lets the agent fully leverage context delegation in the sandbox.
MiniMax Agent’s working filesystem.
Other Computer-use Agents Major AI labs have all been building Computer-use Agents, each guided by its own product philosophy. OpenAI seems to have made great progress on Operator and ChatGPT Atlas. I really enjoy the visual presentation and dynamics of the agent sandbox at runtime.
OpenAI Agent using Terminal
When you prompt Operator to describe its filesystem, you’ll observe a complete Linux VM with two working directories – /home/oai containing session data and /openai storing internal “Skills”. From ChatGPT’s self manifest, the only skill installed is a Browser.
OpenAI Agent filesystem
Beyond OpenAI, numerous teams are pushing here as well. For example, Google AI Studio (Build) can build and test apps from ideas with advanced multimodal capabilities from Gemini, and Manus orchestrates a huge number of subagents and services like browser-use to max out agent action space. I’ll leave the rest of the exploration to readers due to limited time.
Agentic RL Agent, Action (tools) and Environment (scaffold) are three tightly bound concepts in the traditional literature. Consistency between training (rollout) Gym and inference scaffold is critical to maintain agent performance. However, this consistency has become a luxury since model providers won’t expose training infra, usually resulting in tedious engineering efforts guessing the “right” scaffolding and orchestrations among downstream agent builders.
Most LLMs are trained with vanilla sandbox scaffolds to support evaluation like Terminal-Bench, yet a language model doesn’t natively “know” how to use your sandbox – especially when you want to customize the tools and “Skills”. In this case, RL becomes an effective data-efficient method for your last mile.
Rollout Infra One key challenge in Agentic RL is to build stable and efficient rollout infra. The additional factor of sandbox and tools like browser impose difficulty in asynchronous runtime efficiency, state management, and security. These rollouts are usually magnitudes more expensive (time and effort) than non-agentic RL like Math CoT. A nice starting point is OpenHands V1 released lately. The architecture of decoupled modules (abstraction, tools, sandbox, and server) provides solid coordination with reusable modules across scaffolding and rollout serving. Besides, Pytorch OpenEnv provides a nice Gymnasium-style endpoint over commonly used agent docker environments.
OpenHands V1 with decoupled modules reusable across rollout and scaffolding.
Reward Design and Training Recipe Another challenge in Agentic RL is defining the reward function. Computer-use agents usually tackle open-ended tasks like research and app building, which lack unbiased and verifiable scoring mechanisms as in Math and Coding. Meanwhile, vanilla use of LLM-as-judge to generate rewards can easily trap the policy into adversarial distribution from the teacher (Reward Hacking). This is also confirmed in Andrej’s recent interview “RL is terrible”. An interesting approach to mitigate reward hacking in open-ended research is brought by AI2 DR Tulu, where rubrics are buffered and generated on the fly together with policy. The algorithm setups used for Agentic RL generally follow the lessons from long-context RL in LLMs, such as broadening exploration in prolonged steps.
Reinforcement Learning with Evolving Rubrics (RLER)
Conclusion This post showcases the state of Computer-use Agents – from product to design principles. It elaborates the reasoning and importance of runtime sandbox in AI Agents and briefly introduces the engineering and training challenges. 2026 will be the year of Computer-use Agents – with focus shifting from Prompt Engineering to Sandbox Engineering and RL on custom scaffolds ;)
Citation Xu, Binfeng. "Demystifying Agent Sandbox". B'Log (Dec 2025). https://billxbf.github.io/posts/demystify-agent-sandbox/
summary:
Modern AI agents are typically scaffolded with a runtime sandbox, and these Computer-Use Agents (CUA) autonomously run code, use the terminal, take notes, and access the Internet and MCPs – exactly like humans do when interacting with the digital world.

Yet the underlying reasoning and practices remain unclear to most, so let’s dive into popular agent scaffolds like Claude Code and MiniMax Agent, demystifying the design principles and discovering how agents benefit from using a computer.
title: Demystifying Agent Sandbox

Production AI agents are usually packed with bloated tool descriptions and system prompts to cover more case handling and example following. Context length can easily grow to 100k or over 1M in multi-turn agentic trajectories – especially when tool responses contain extraneous data. Growing contexts dilute attention and cause huge memory burden to both training and inference.
Intuitively, you’d want to move episodic context out of the main agent loop using:
Subagent: Spin up subtasks to invoke another agent. Without sharing a full context, subagents can easily duplicate work, wasting excessive tokens, while over-engineered orchestration introduces inductive bias. Filesystem: Use the filesystem to keep todo lists, agent memory, and conditional instructions (aka Agent Skill) that the agent chooses to load into the context when necessary. Sandbox Filesystem is an agent’s extended context through computer-use. Runtime Isolation. Sandboxing keeps all agent actions in a tightly controlled environment, protecting the host system by isolating potential errors and de-risking real user data or resources. That isolation is especially critical when executing popular <python> and <browser> tools.
Cursor (Agent mode) running commands in a secure sandbox
Claude Code – More than just Code Claude Code has gained huge traction in 2025. It was originally built for coding assistance, but Anthropic is clearly steering it toward more general agent use cases. As highlighted in recent podcasts, Claude Code already excels at, or can be extended to, deep research and vertical specialist agents. Andrej has also been tweeting mini projects like agentic home control in the physical world.
Claude Code scaffolding. The agent controls a virtual sandbox with Bash and coding. Context delegated to filesystem (Skills). Tools executed outside the sandbox are implemented as MCP.
Agentic Computer Use What’s the magic here? Metaphorically, an AI Agent equipped with a Filesystem and Runtime Environment is just a human using a computer – the primary, if not only interface connecting humans to the Digital World. Imagine the action space. ¯_(ツ)_/¯ With that lens, scaffolding an agent turns into OS design:
Configuring a Linux Docker / VM, or even creating a completely native OS from scratch (yes, there are a few working on it). Designing the Filesystem within the sandbox. Pre-install some “Apps” for the agents – a Terminal, a Browser, a writing pad … and place some instructions for the agent to refer to before use (Anthropic names it SKILL.md). Meanwhile, give agents a “shortcut” to invoke outside endpoints – like credential-related database querying. These fit into the bucket of traditional function-calling or MCP. Claude Code System Prompt Anthropic still doesn’t publicly share Claude Code’s system prompt and tool implementations in its Claude Agent SDK. However, there’s an interesting thread where people trace and hack Claude Code’s request & response, and reverse engineer the system prompt and its commit diff across versions. From that prompt, we can clearly find some sparks and lessons in agent tool implementations.
For example, Claude Code doesn’t use an interactive browser like browser-use does by default. Instead it splits capabilities into separate WebSearch and WebFetch tools, likely due to speed & efficiency concerns.
Summary of Claude Code’s tools from the hacked system prompt.
Claude Code Filesystem When locally deployed, Claude Code uses ~/.claude/ for its own sandbox workspace, and it mounts your work project under ~/.claude/projects/. The scaffold keeps plugins (MCP integrations) and skills in different directories, and also tracks TODO, personalization configs, and metadata like command history and debug logs.
Claude Code’s working filesystem.
MiniMax Agent MiniMax has been the most surprising AI lab for me this year. It ships the #1 (as of Dec 2025) OSS model on LMArena WebDev at 229B weights, which is considered “small” among parallel flagship models. Meanwhile, the agent scaffold, MiniMax Agent creates surprisingly good apps and reports with prolonged reasoning and autonomous execution. Here’s my favorite trajectory replay.
MiniMax Agent using browser for autonomous app creation (Netflix Clone)
MiniMax Agent has a UI component that lets you navigate the agent’s sandbox filesystem in realtime, and you can prompt the agent to summarize its initial filesystem state. The workspace is a Python-based development environment designed for AI agents with integrated external API access capabilities. Rich modular data sources connect to various third-party APIs including Twitter, Yahoo Finance, TripAdvisor, Pinterest, Patents, Scholar, Commodities, Metals, and Booking.com. None of these live inside the system prompt, which lets the agent fully leverage context delegation in the sandbox.
MiniMax Agent’s working filesystem.
Other Computer-use Agents Major AI labs have all been building Computer-use Agents, each guided by its own product philosophy. OpenAI seems to have made great progress on Operator and ChatGPT Atlas. I really enjoy the visual presentation and dynamics of the agent sandbox at runtime.
OpenAI Agent using Terminal
When you prompt Operator to describe its filesystem, you’ll observe a complete Linux VM with two working directories – /home/oai containing session data and /openai storing internal “Skills”. From ChatGPT’s self manifest, the only skill installed is a Browser.
OpenAI Agent filesystem
Beyond OpenAI, numerous teams are pushing here as well. For example, Google AI Studio (Build) can build and test apps from ideas with advanced multimodal capabilities from Gemini, and Manus orchestrates a huge number of subagents and services like browser-use to max out agent action space. I’ll leave the rest of the exploration to readers due to limited time.
Agentic RL Agent, Action (tools) and Environment (scaffold) are three tightly bound concepts in the traditional literature. Consistency between training (rollout) Gym and inference scaffold is critical to maintain agent performance. However, this consistency has become a luxury since model providers won’t expose training infra, usually resulting in tedious engineering efforts guessing the “right” scaffolding and orchestrations among downstream agent builders.
Most LLMs are trained with vanilla sandbox scaffolds to support evaluation like Terminal-Bench, yet a language model doesn’t natively “know” how to use your sandbox – especially when you want to customize the tools and “Skills”. In this case, RL becomes an effective data-efficient method for your last mile.
Rollout Infra One key challenge in Agentic RL is to build stable and efficient rollout infra. The additional factor of sandbox and tools like browser impose difficulty in asynchronous runtime efficiency, state management, and security. These rollouts are usually magnitudes more expensive (time and effort) than non-agentic RL like Math CoT. A nice starting point is OpenHands V1 released lately. The architecture of decoupled modules (abstraction, tools, sandbox, and server) provides solid coordination with reusable modules across scaffolding and rollout serving. Besides, Pytorch OpenEnv provides a nice Gymnasium-style endpoint over commonly used agent docker environments.
OpenHands V1 with decoupled modules reusable across rollout and scaffolding.
Reward Design and Training Recipe Another challenge in Agentic RL is defining the reward function. Computer-use agents usually tackle open-ended tasks like research and app building, which lack unbiased and verifiable scoring mechanisms as in Math and Coding. Meanwhile, vanilla use of LLM-as-judge to generate rewards can easily trap the policy into adversarial distribution from the teacher (Reward Hacking). This is also confirmed in Andrej’s recent interview “RL is terrible”. An interesting approach to mitigate reward hacking in open-ended research is brought by AI2 DR Tulu, where rubrics are buffered and generated on the fly together with policy. The algorithm setups used for Agentic RL generally follow the lessons from long-context RL in LLMs, such as broadening exploration in prolonged steps.
Reinforcement Learning with Evolving Rubrics (RLER)
Conclusion This post showcases the state of Computer-use Agents – from product to design principles. It elaborates the reasoning and importance of runtime sandbox in AI Agents and briefly introduces the engineering and training challenges. 2026 will be the year of Computer-use Agents – with focus shifting from Prompt Engineering to Sandbox Engineering and RL on custom scaffolds ;)
Citation Xu, Binfeng. "Demystifying Agent Sandbox". B'Log (Dec 2025). https://billxbf.github.io/posts/demystify-agent-sandbox/
summary:
Modern AI agents are typically scaffolded with a runtime sandbox, and these Computer-Use Agents (CUA) autonomously run code, use the terminal, take notes, and access the Internet and MCPs – exactly like humans do when interacting with the digital world.

Yet the underlying reasoning and practices remain unclear to most, so let’s dive into popular agent scaffolds like Claude Code and MiniMax Agent, demystifying the design principles and discovering how agents benefit from using a computer.
title: Demystifying Agent Sandbox

content:
This is a fairly procrastinated start to my personal blog. Starting a blog isn’t as easy as it seems—I don’t want to waste people’s time with casual anecdotes. Meanwhile, an overly formal academic write-up would likely be overkill and scare people away.
There are many people who truly enjoy machine learning and find joy in sharing knowledge. I’ve been a long-time follower of AI/tech blogs from Andrej Karpathy, Lilian Weng, Yao Fu, and others. I usually prefer blogs over papers because blogs feel more honest and less AI-polished (or written to attract citations). Yet almost everyone I followed stopped posting in early 2025. I understand the shifts and hype in SF lately that keep everyone busy building and/or financially free. Still, I’d be sad if this vibe disappears—it’s been truly helpful to me over the past few years, along with many others.
I’ve been more of a builder than a writer, hesitant to publish conclusions without solid ground. But lately I’ve found it really fruitful to do deep dives into the state of things and the literature before engineering. A blog of “study notes” not only helps me organize thoughts, but also carries on that missing vibe of sharing knowledge in a blunt and unpretentious way. And so I finally made up my mind to start writing—and I truly hope you enjoy it. Stay tuned and keep the spark going!
What to Expect I’ll be writing about:
Tech Hacks: Most of the time, truth in tech world doesn’t reside in reports or posts but hidden in code. I enjoy digging these gems out to see the real flow. Different AI Research: I continuously keep track of frontier AI research and especially favor the non-incremental ones, like new architectures to model intelligence. Stories and Gossip: The true SOTA usually come from stories and gossip shared through friends, meetups, and conferences.

title: Why I start to write

content:
Binfeng Xu I’m a research engineer at NVIDIA. Currently, I work on agent harness and RL for computer-use and continual learning.
Formerly, I was a researcher at Samsung Research (SRA) where I led LLM post-training + distillation infra. I enjoy training large neural nets, building open-source projects and competing on Kaggle, where I rank top 1% globally.
Papers Gentopia: A Collaborative Platform for Tool-Augmented LLMs
Binfeng Xu, Xukun Liu, Hua Shen, Zeyu H, Yuhan L, Murong Y, Zhiyuan P, Yuchen L, Ziyu Y, Dongkuan Xu
ReWOO: Decoupling Reasoning from Observations for Efficient Augmented Language Models
Binfeng Xu, Zhiyuan Peng, Bowen Lei, Subhabrata Mukherjee, Yuchen Liu, Dongkuan Xu
Dynamic Noise Preference Optimization for LLM Self-Improvement
Haoyan Yang, Ting Hua, Shangqian Gao, Binfeng Xu, Zheng Tang, Jie Xu, Hongxia Jin, Vijay Srinivasan
Efficient Computation of Tucker Decomposition of Correlation-Based Tensors
Binfeng Xu, Grey Ballard, Robert Lyday, Paul Laurienti
Misc I play all games by Hidetaka Miyazaki, who motivated me once into indie Game Dev. Photography @Instagram; I enjoy Art. Minimalist.

Binfeng Xu

Formerly, I was a researcher at Samsung Research (SRA) where I led LLM post-training + distillation infra. I enjoy training large neural nets, building open-source projects and competing on Kaggle, where I rank top 1% globally.

  • Gentopia: A Collaborative Platform for Tool-Augmented LLMs

content:
For most of the past two years, “RL for LLMs” effectively meant RLVR – a model writes one response, an automatic verifier scores it, and the trainer updates the policy. Math, coding, and short reasoning all fit that mold. The whole stack – vLLM/SGLang for rollout, verl/OpenRLHF for training – was built around the assumption that a rollout is essentially one forward pass with a reward stapled to the end.
That assumption no longer holds. The frontier has moved from single-shot reasoning to long-running, multi-turn, environment-bound agents: software-engineering agents editing repositories, browser agents navigating the web, computer-use agents living inside a Linux VM, multi-agent systems delegating to sub-agents. The rollout is no longer a token sequence; it’s a session that opens a sandbox, calls tools dozens of times, manages its own context, and may persist for minutes to hours.
The natural reaction is to bolt agentic support onto an existing RLVR framework. The 2026 reality, after half a year of trying that myself and watching every major lab publish their own redesign, is that this no longer works – not as an algorithmic limitation but as a systems limitation. The four most prominent recent works – MiniMax’s Forge, Alibaba’s ROLL, Moonshot’s Seer, and Zhipu’s slime – all converge on the same diagnosis even though they pick different remedies. Combined with my own work at NVIDIA on Polar, this post is an attempt to synthesize what the paradigm shift actually demands of infra, and where we should be heading.
Placeholder: a side-by-side diagram contrasting the RLVR loop (prompt → response → reward → update) with the agentic RL loop (instruction → multi-turn harness execution against a live runtime → trajectory → reward → update). Highlight the proportions of compute spent in each stage.
What actually changed It’s tempting to summarize the shift as “trajectories got longer.” That undersells it. Three deeper things have changed, each of which independently breaks a load-bearing assumption in current RL stacks.
The agent now lives outside the framework. In RLVR, the rollout function was a Python method owned by the trainer. It packed the prompt, decoded a response, and handed back a tensor. In agentic RL, the trajectory is produced by an agent harness – often a separate process or a closed-source binary like Claude Code or Codex. The trainer must accept that the agent is no longer something it implements, but something it listens to. Forge captured this directly in their definition of an Agent as a “pure trajectory producer,” and ROLL went a step further with their CLI-Native Mode where the training framework hands context management completely over to the agent’s own runtime (e.g. iFlow CLI). This sounds like a small refactor and turns out to be a fundamental redesign of who owns state.
Rollout is 80%+ of the wall clock, and the tail dominates. ROLL’s own technical report puts rollout at roughly 70% of post-training cost. In my own runs, I have seen it cross 90%. The reasons are mundane and unavoidable: an agent episode pays for tool latency, sandbox startup, file I/O, network round-trips, sub-agent retries, and context compaction in addition to LLM token generation. Worse, the variance is enormous – some episodes finish in 5 seconds, others run for 40 minutes. A single slow episode in a group-based sampling regime like GRPO stalls 15 perfectly-finished siblings. The old “barrier-synchronized rollout, then a barrier-synchronized update” loop simply cannot survive this distribution.
Reward signal travels through a long, leaky chain. In RLVR, the verifier was a pure function. In agentic RL the reward is computed via agent action → environment execution → test verification → reward returned. Every link can be gamed. ROLL has publicly reported observing false-positive rates as high as 40% in early synthetic instance pools – the agent “passed” a test it never actually solved. The standard infra response of “just write a stricter checker” is wrong; the chain itself is now part of the optimization surface, and infra must treat it as such.
These three together explain why every major lab spent 2025 effectively rebuilding their post-training stack from scratch. Not because the algorithms were wrong – GRPO and DAPO and GSPO are all still recognizable – but because the system shape underneath them was wrong.
The new boundary: agent as a black box The first architectural decision that every modern agentic RL system has to make is where to draw the line between the trainer and the agent. Three choices have emerged.
The naive option, which is what most early agentic RL frameworks did, is white-box integration: the framework subclasses an agent, implements its tool loop, and owns the multi-turn buffer. This is the SkyRL / OpenHands gymnasium pattern. It works when the agent is simple and you control its source. It fails the moment the agent is a closed-source product (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Devin) or has a non-trivial internal control loop (sub-agents, context compaction, MCP servers, cron-like memory jobs). Even when the agent is open-source, the framework has to chase the agent’s internal API on every release.
The middle option is structured instrumentation: Agent Lightning and rLLM both go this way, exposing tracing hooks and a unified data interface so any agent can be made trainable with minimal code change. This lowers the integration cost but still asks the agent to conform to the trainer’s contract.
The endpoint option – and the one I believe is the right long-term answer – is to move the integration boundary all the way down to the LLM API endpoint itself. Every LLM-based agent, no matter how exotic its internal scaffolding, must eventually call a model. If you proxy that call, you can train without touching the agent at all. This is what Forge does with its Gateway Server, what Slime does with its TITO Gateway, and what Polar does with its harness-as-blackbox proxy.
Placeholder: a stack diagram showing three integration boundaries – (1) white-box agent class inside framework, (2) instrumentation hooks via SDK/decorators, (3) LLM API proxy below the harness. Annotate which existing systems sit at each boundary.
This boundary choice has a deeply non-obvious consequence that I want to flag explicitly, because it bit me hard: Token-In-Token-Out (TITO) fidelity. Once your trainer no longer owns the agent’s tool-call formatting, prompt rendering, and context compaction, the tokens that get trained on and the tokens that were actually sampled at rollout time can diverge. A model that re-tokenizes a tool-call JSON, or that decodes a streamed response and then re-encodes it, will produce off-by-one tokens that silently corrupt the importance ratio. Forge and slime both elevate TITO to a first-class design constraint, recording raw sampled token IDs and log probabilities from the inference backend rather than reconstructing them from text after the fact. Polar takes the same stance and propagates raw token_ids, loss_mask, and response_logprobs end-to-end from the proxy. If your trainer is recovering tokens by re-encoding text, you have a silent bug right now.
Rollout, in detail If there is one section of this post worth lingering on, it is this one. Every infra problem in agentic RL pools at the rollout stage, and the design space here is genuinely rich.
Why agentic rollout naturally goes async In RLVR, although individual rollouts can be slow, the task structure is regular: one generation, one reward. The whole pipeline is expensive but uniform. In agentic RL the variance explodes for three compounding reasons.
First, an agent episode is not just token generation. It includes tool dispatch, sandbox execution, network latency, file I/O, sub-agent retries, and multi-turn context bookkeeping. Two episodes with similar token counts can finish in dramatically different wall times.
Second, long-tail samples are the norm, not the exception. Some episodes finish in seconds; others get stuck in retry loops or slow tools and crawl into the tens of minutes. Reward sparsity makes this worse: the most informative episodes are often the longest.
Third, group-based sampling amplifies the tail. GRPO needs K responses to one prompt. If you co-schedule them on one node, a single tail sample holds the entire group hostage. The Seer team identified this specifically as the dominant source of GPU idle time in synchronous group rollout.
A strict barrier-synchronous rollout therefore exposes every tail as a system-wide stall. The natural reaction is to go async, but async is not free.
The async-vs-sync spectrum Each of the four prominent recent systems takes a different position on this spectrum, and reading them together is more useful than reading any of them alone. The Forge blog gives the unifying objective:
$$\max_\theta J(\theta) = \text{Throughput}(\mathcal{A}) \times \text{SampleEfficiency}(\mathcal{A})$$
subject to stability and convergence constraints. Every design choice is a tradeoff between these two terms. Going aggressively async multiplies throughput but tanks sample efficiency (off-policy drift); staying strictly sync preserves sample efficiency but tanks throughput. The four systems each find a different point on this curve.
Forge – Windowed FIFO. Forge refuses both extremes. It defines a window $[H, H+W]$ over the head of the trajectory queue and allows local out-of-order consumption inside the window. Trajectories outside the window cannot be skipped. The result is bounded staleness with controlled sample distribution – not strict FIFO, but not greedy async either. The point of Windowed FIFO is not scheduling; it is sample distribution control.
ROLL – fully async pipeline with multiplexing. ROLL goes the most aggressive engineering route. It decomposes the RL iteration into generation, environment interaction, and reward computation as independently schedulable stages, plumbs them through a sample buffer, and adds an asynchronous_ratio to bound how many policy versions can sit between a sample’s generation and its consumption. On top of that, ROLL introduces train-rollout multiplexing: GPUs dynamically reallocate between rollout and training depending on which side is starving. The price tag is operational complexity – stale-sample handling, mask/filter/reweight, rollback/resume.
slime – fully async + token-level clipping. Slime takes the same async stance but pushes off-policy correction down to the token level. Rather than reconstructing exact historical policy distributions per token, it reuses log-probs recorded at rollout time and applies direct double-sided importance clipping $[1-\epsilon_l, 1+\epsilon_h]$; tokens outside the band are simply masked from the gradient. At the sample level it adds a staleness threshold and drops samples that span too many policy versions. The philosophy is “give up exact $\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}$ bookkeeping; engineer your way around the most dangerous off-policy tokens instead.”
Seer – stay synchronous, but make the tail thinner. Seer is the contrarian. Instead of accepting off-policy drift in exchange for throughput, it asks: can we keep strict sync and still kill the tail? Their answer has three parts:
Divided Rollout: split a GRPO group into independently-schedulable requests, then split each request into 8K-token chunks. The scheduling unit becomes a chunk, not a group. Context-Aware Scheduling: run one speculative request per group as a length probe, then order the rest using longest-first heuristics from that probe. Adaptive Grouped Speculative Decoding: instead of training a static draft model, mine the in-flight intra-group response history for repeated suffix patterns and use those as draft tokens. This is model-free speculative decoding that gets better as the group fills in. There is no single right answer here. If you can afford the engineering complexity and your workload tolerates off-policyness (most coding agents do), ROLL/slime-style async wins on throughput. If you are running a synchronously-trained model in a regime where off-policy drift hurts (RLHF-flavored alignment, short-horizon tasks), Seer’s chunked-sync approach is genuinely competitive.
Placeholder: a horizontal spectrum chart with strict-sync on the left and fully-async on the right. Place Seer, Forge (Windowed FIFO), ROLL, and slime on the spectrum, and annotate the throughput × sample-efficiency tradeoff for each.
Prefix redundancy: the silent compute drain There is a separate, often-ignored axis of waste in agentic rollout that’s worth calling out before we move on: shared prefixes. A single agentic trajectory contains many repeated long prefixes – multi-turn histories that grow by appending, GRPO groups that share the same initial prompt, sub-agent calls that share a parent context. Treating each completion as an independent sequence means re-running attention over those shared prefixes multiple times.
Forge’s Prefix Tree Merging and AReaL’s Dynamic Tree Attention both attack this directly, computing shared prefixes once and forking only at branch points. On the inference side, Forge and Seer both argue for a global L3 KV cache pool to make these savings visible across instances rather than per-replica. In Polar’s own experiments, switching from per_request reconstruction to prefix_merging cut wall-clock training time by 5.39× under identical configuration, with rollout GPU utilization climbing from 20% to 88%. Prefix redundancy is no longer a micro-optimization; in modern agentic RL it is the difference between “the trainer is mostly idle” and “the trainer is mostly busy.”
Polar: a black-box rollout substrate This is the section where I bring in my own work. Polar (the paper I just released with the NeMo team) is a rollout framework that takes the harness-agnostic stance seriously: it never opens the agent.
The core observation is the same one above: every LLM-based agent must eventually call a model API. If we sit a provider-compatible proxy at exactly that boundary – understanding Anthropic Messages, OpenAI Chat Completions, OpenAI Responses, and Google generateContent – we can listen to the entire conversation without touching the harness. The proxy detects the provider, normalizes the request to the local inference shape (logprobs=true added), forwards it to SGLang or vLLM, records token IDs and log probabilities, and translates the response back into whatever schema the harness expected. From the harness’s point of view nothing happened. From the trainer’s point of view, a token-faithful trajectory dropped onto disk.
Placeholder: redraw Figure 1 from the Polar paper – rollout server with session dispatch, gateway nodes with INIT/RUN/BUILD/EVAL worker pools, the API proxy capturing token-level traces, and async callbacks back to the trainer. Highlight that the harness is unmodified.
Two design choices follow from this boundary placement.
Rollout-as-a-service with staged async. A long-horizon agent rollout mixes very different costs: runtime startup (CPU, slow), dependency preparation (CPU, slow), harness execution (GPU-bound, variable), evaluator setup (CPU, slow), patch application and teardown (CPU, slow). If these run serially per session, the GPU spends most of its time idle. Polar’s gateway nodes therefore split execution into three isolated worker pools – INIT, RUNNING, POSTRUN – plus a bounded READY buffer. While one session is running, the next session’s runtime is already prewarming, and the previous session’s evaluator is already running post-hoc in POSTRUN. The async staging is inside a gateway node, not across the cluster, which keeps the trainer-side contract simple: submit a task, receive a callback.
Token-faithful prefix merging. The naive trace builder emits one trainer-facing sample per captured completion (per_request). For a complex coding harness that calls the model 50+ times in one session, this fragments a single coherent trajectory into 50 short, separately-credited samples. Polar’s prefix_merging recovers append-only conversation chains by checking, for adjacent completions $C_{i_m}$ and $C_{i_{m+1}}$, whether the strict token-prefix relation holds:
$$p_{i_{m+1}}[1:|p_{i_m}|] = p_{i_m}$$
When it does, the completions are merged into one trace with sampled assistant tokens marked trainable in the loss mask and canonical interstitial tokens masked out. Sub-agents, parallel branches, and compaction events naturally form their own chains. The result is fewer, longer, semantically-coherent trajectories – the 5.39× speedup mentioned above, and crucially, a credit-assignment unit that actually corresponds to a session. (We also tried per-request with outcome-reward broadcasting; the trainer immediately reward-hacked. Session granularity is not just an optimization; it’s a correctness condition.)
In SWE-Bench Verified evaluation, the same Qwen3.5-4B base model goes from 3.8% → 26.4% under the Codex harness, 29.8% → 34.6% under Claude Code, 34.6% → 35.2% under Qwen Code, and 34.2% → 40.4% under the Pi harness, all without modifying any harness. The largest jumps come from harnesses whose action protocol is most unfamiliar to the base policy, which is exactly the regime where harness-native RL should matter most.
Lessons from actually running this Theory is cheap. Half a year of running agent RL has produced more concrete operational lessons than any framework comparison can. The following five are the ones I wish someone had handed me on day one – they all cost me at least a week of compute before I figured them out.
1. Sparse rewards win. Shaping helps. Unbounded shaping kills. Pure 0/1 outcome rewards barely move the policy in cold start. On one code agent, pure outcome training over 5,000 steps moved success from 8% to 12% – statistically distinguishable, practically a loss. Adding process-reward shaping (small intermediate-checkpoint bonuses) lifted the same setup to 31% over the same step budget. But here’s the catch: every component of the shaping reward must have a hard cap. I cap each intermediate checkpoint at +0.1, the total process reward at +0.5, and leave the outcome reward to dominate. Without a cap, any positive intermediate signal becomes a hacking surface.
One concrete cautionary tale: another team added a penalty on tool-call count to encourage parsimony. The model learned to do all the work in a long <thinking> block and then fabricate the final answer with zero tool calls. The reward looked great. The success rate cratered. Any unbounded reward, positive or negative, is a hack channel. Treat reward design as adversarial.
2. Watch throughput, not loss. Agent RL spends 90% of its time waiting on environments. Staring at the loss curve while rollout is the bottleneck is the most common newbie mistake. The three highest-impact rollout speedups I’ve seen are not algorithmic:
Sandbox container pooling. Switching from one-sandbox-per-rollout to a pool of 64 reusable sandboxes gave a 3.5× throughput improvement on its own. Verl async mode with generation_lag=2~4. That’s the sweet spot. Anything above 6 lags and the KL between the rollout policy and the training policy blows up. Speculative rollout pre-warming. Pre-generate the next batch’s first turn while the current batch’s reward is being computed. For KL control specifically: avoid fixed-beta penalty. An adaptive KL controller targeting target_kl=0.05 is roughly a full order of magnitude more stable than fixed beta=0.04 in long-trajectory regimes. The longer the trajectory, the more this matters.
3. Observation masking is only the basement floor of multi-turn losses. Everyone knows you mask observations. The three subtler traps:
Mask tool error messages too. Otherwise the model learns to echo errors as a cheap way to pad sequence length and farm length-related reward signal. Never mask thinking tokens. That’s where the model’s reasoning value actually lives. Use DAPO’s token-level loss aggregation, not sequence-level, under long CoT. The difference is roughly 30% more stable training in my runs. For credit assignment, a turn-wise value head with GAE($\lambda=0.95$) converges almost 2× faster than sharing one trajectory-level advantage across all turns. And one important correction to popular practice: GRPO uses PPO-style token-level importance ratios. GSPO is the variant that lifts the importance ratio to the sequence level, and it is visibly more stable under MoE + long-trajectory workloads. The choice between them is not academic.
4. Naked GRPO has known traps; the fixes are concrete. On one web agent I trained with vanilla GRPO for 200 steps. Average response length climbed from 800 to 2400 tokens. Accuracy didn’t move. This is the classic length bias – GRPO’s length-normalized advantage rewards verbose outputs that happen to fall above the group mean.
Two fixes that work:
Dr.GRPO: drop length-averaging and std-normalization to recover an unbiased baseline. DAPO Clip-Higher: raise clip_upper from 0.2 to 0.28 to give low-probability tokens room to explore. For entropy collapse: don’t watch the loss curve, watch a rollout distinct-3 diversity metric. Once it drops below 0.3, switch on a dynamic entropy bonus – I start at 0.001 and linearly decay to 0.0001 over 100 steps. This catches collapse far earlier than any loss-based signal.
5. Reward functions need red-teaming, not just verification. This one is the most important and the most consistently underestimated. Before any reward function goes live, hand it to a separate model and let it hack the reward for 10,000 steps. Four hack modes will almost always surface:
Tool spam – call tools at the maximum rate to farm any per-call signal. Echo prompt – copy ground-truth keywords from the prompt into the response. Ground-truth keyword stuffing – if a string-match grader is anywhere in the chain, the model finds it. Format padding – bloat the structural scaffolding of the response to look more “complete.” Concrete example: a SQL agent gave 0.3 partial credit for “syntactically correct SQL.” The model promptly learned to emit SELECT * FROM <random_field_name> and gamble on schema matches. Changing the criterion to “executes successfully AND row count matches” eliminated the hack overnight.
In production training, sample 50 rollouts every 500 steps and run an LLM-as-judge for hack-pattern detection. This catches drift far more reliably than monitoring the reward curve, because a clean reward curve is exactly what a successful hack produces.
This connects directly back to ROLL’s environmental-cleanliness work and Polar’s outcome-reward broadcasting failure I mentioned earlier. Reward integrity is an infrastructure problem. It is not solved at the algorithm layer.
Placeholder: a 2x2 grid illustrating the four common reward-hacking modes (tool spam, prompt echo, ground-truth keyword stuffing, format padding), each with a short before/after example.
What the next-gen RL infra should look like Pulling the threads together, here is what I think a modern agentic RL stack must provide as first-class primitives. Most current frameworks do one or two of these well; almost none do all of them.
1. A model-API integration boundary. Stop subclassing agents. Stop instrumenting harnesses. Put a multi-provider proxy at the LLM API and capture token IDs and log probabilities at that boundary. Polar, Forge’s Gateway Server, and slime’s TITO Gateway all settled here independently for good reason.
2. Rollout as a service, not as a function. A rollout is a long-running session with its own lifecycle (init, run, post-run, teardown), not a function call returning a tensor. Expose it as an async task API with callbacks, separate gateway nodes from training, and let CPU-heavy setup and GPU-bound generation overlap inside each gateway.
3. Explicit staleness and policy-version tracking. If you go async (and most workloads should), the policy version that generated a sample must travel with the sample. Stale samples beyond a threshold get dropped or down-weighted, not silently consumed. ROLL’s asynchronous_ratio and slime’s per-sample version stamp are the right shape.
4. Token-faithful traces with prefix merging. Trainer-facing traces must carry the exact token IDs and log probabilities that flowed through the inference server. And shared prefixes must merge – both for compute efficiency and for credit assignment to land on session-coherent boundaries.
5. Chunked credit assignment. Token-level credit assignment is too fine for agentic tasks (most tokens don’t change environment state). Trajectory-level is too coarse. The natural unit is the interaction chunk – a span between two environment interactions, typically delimited by a tool call. ROLL’s Chunked MDP / IPA formalizes this and it’s the right abstraction.
6. Environment hygiene as part of the trainer contract. Clean the runtime before every rollout. Hide test files from the agent during training. Filter instances where the golden solution fails the test, and instances where a no-op passes the test. Add controlled environment diversity (different package versions, different image sources, occasionally-broken dependencies) so the policy learns to inspect and adapt rather than memorize. Treat false-positive rate as a first-class metric.
7. A reward red-team in the training loop. Run a separate adversarial model against every reward function before launch. Sample rollouts periodically with an LLM-as-judge for hack-pattern detection. Cap every shaping component. Assume reward functions are buggy by default.
None of these are individually novel. The point is that they need to be present together, as a coherent stack, rather than as scattered ad-hoc patches over a framework that was designed for a fundamentally different workload.
Closing “RL framework” used to mean a tight loop around generate → reward → update. It is becoming something much larger – a distributed system that connects a continuously-updating policy to an arbitrary external runtime, across version drift, environment noise, tail latency, and adversarial reward channels. The training algorithm is increasingly the smallest part of the problem.
What I find genuinely exciting about this moment is that the field is finally taking that observation seriously. Forge, ROLL, Seer, slime, and Polar are all, in different ways, arguments that the systems design now matters more than the algorithm choice. I expect 2026 will be the year where this becomes consensus – and the labs that ship the cleanest abstractions will ship the most capable agents. ;)
Citation Xu, Binfeng. "Rethinking RL Infra for Agents". B'Log (May 2026). https://billxbf.github.io/posts/agent-rl-infra/ References Xu, Binfeng et al. Polar: Agentic RL on Any Harness at Scale. NVIDIA, 2026. [arXiv] [code] MiniMax. Forge: A Unified Agentic RL Framework. 2026. Alibaba. ROLL: Reinforcement Learning Optimization for Large Language Models. 2025. Moonshot. Seer: Synchronous Agentic RL with Online Context-Aware Scheduling. 2026. Zhipu. slime: An LLM Post-Training Framework for RL Scaling. 2025. [code]
summary: Why agentic RL is breaking the assumptions that RLVR frameworks were built on – and what the next generation of RL infra needs to look like. A survey of Forge, ROLL, Seer and Slime, plus lessons from my own work on Polar.
title: Rethinking RL Infra for Agents

content:
Modern AI agents are typically scaffolded with a runtime sandbox, and these Computer-Use Agents (CUA) autonomously run code, use the terminal, take notes, and access the Internet and MCPs – exactly like humans do when interacting with the digital world.
Yet the underlying reasoning and practices remain unclear to most, so let’s dive into popular agent scaffolds like Claude Code and MiniMax Agent, demystifying the design principles and discovering how agents benefit from using a computer.
Why Sandbox? In short, Context Delegation and Runtime Isolation.
Context Delegation. Even with memory-efficient tricks like KV caching and Linear Attention architectures, long-context reasoning is still a nontrivial challenge to AI agents. Production AI agents are usually packed with bloated tool descriptions and system prompts to cover more case handling and example following. Context length can easily grow to 100k or over 1M in multi-turn agentic trajectories – especially when tool responses contain extraneous data. Growing contexts dilute attention and cause huge memory burden to both training and inference.
Intuitively, you’d want to move episodic context out of the main agent loop using:
Subagent: Spin up subtasks to invoke another agent. Without sharing a full context, subagents can easily duplicate work, wasting excessive tokens, while over-engineered orchestration introduces inductive bias. Filesystem: Use the filesystem to keep todo lists, agent memory, and conditional instructions (aka Agent Skill) that the agent chooses to load into the context when necessary. Sandbox Filesystem is an agent’s extended context through computer-use. Runtime Isolation. Sandboxing keeps all agent actions in a tightly controlled environment, protecting the host system by isolating potential errors and de-risking real user data or resources. That isolation is especially critical when executing popular <python> and <browser> tools.
Cursor (Agent mode) running commands in a secure sandbox
Claude Code – More than just Code Claude Code has gained huge traction in 2025. It was originally built for coding assistance, but Anthropic is clearly steering it toward more general agent use cases. As highlighted in recent podcasts, Claude Code already excels at, or can be extended to, deep research and vertical specialist agents. Andrej has also been tweeting mini projects like agentic home control in the physical world.
Claude Code scaffolding. The agent controls a virtual sandbox with Bash and coding. Context delegated to filesystem (Skills). Tools executed outside the sandbox are implemented as MCP.
Agentic Computer Use What’s the magic here? Metaphorically, an AI Agent equipped with a Filesystem and Runtime Environment is just a human using a computer – the primary, if not only interface connecting humans to the Digital World. Imagine the action space. ¯_(ツ)_/¯ With that lens, scaffolding an agent turns into OS design:
Configuring a Linux Docker / VM, or even creating a completely native OS from scratch (yes, there are a few working on it). Designing the Filesystem within the sandbox. Pre-install some “Apps” for the agents – a Terminal, a Browser, a writing pad … and place some instructions for the agent to refer to before use (Anthropic names it SKILL.md). Meanwhile, give agents a “shortcut” to invoke outside endpoints – like credential-related database querying. These fit into the bucket of traditional function-calling or MCP. Claude Code System Prompt Anthropic still doesn’t publicly share Claude Code’s system prompt and tool implementations in its Claude Agent SDK. However, there’s an interesting thread where people trace and hack Claude Code’s request & response, and reverse engineer the system prompt and its commit diff across versions. From that prompt, we can clearly find some sparks and lessons in agent tool implementations.
For example, Claude Code doesn’t use an interactive browser like browser-use does by default. Instead it splits capabilities into separate WebSearch and WebFetch tools, likely due to speed & efficiency concerns.
Summary of Claude Code’s tools from the hacked system prompt.
Claude Code Filesystem When locally deployed, Claude Code uses ~/.claude/ for its own sandbox workspace, and it mounts your work project under ~/.claude/projects/. The scaffold keeps plugins (MCP integrations) and skills in different directories, and also tracks TODO, personalization configs, and metadata like command history and debug logs.
Claude Code’s working filesystem.
MiniMax Agent MiniMax has been the most surprising AI lab for me this year. It ships the #1 (as of Dec 2025) OSS model on LMArena WebDev at 229B weights, which is considered “small” among parallel flagship models. Meanwhile, the agent scaffold, MiniMax Agent creates surprisingly good apps and reports with prolonged reasoning and autonomous execution. Here’s my favorite trajectory replay.
MiniMax Agent using browser for autonomous app creation (Netflix Clone)
MiniMax Agent has a UI component that lets you navigate the agent’s sandbox filesystem in realtime, and you can prompt the agent to summarize its initial filesystem state. The workspace is a Python-based development environment designed for AI agents with integrated external API access capabilities. Rich modular data sources connect to various third-party APIs including Twitter, Yahoo Finance, TripAdvisor, Pinterest, Patents, Scholar, Commodities, Metals, and Booking.com. None of these live inside the system prompt, which lets the agent fully leverage context delegation in the sandbox.
MiniMax Agent’s working filesystem.
Other Computer-use Agents Major AI labs have all been building Computer-use Agents, each guided by its own product philosophy. OpenAI seems to have made great progress on Operator and ChatGPT Atlas. I really enjoy the visual presentation and dynamics of the agent sandbox at runtime.
OpenAI Agent using Terminal
When you prompt Operator to describe its filesystem, you’ll observe a complete Linux VM with two working directories – /home/oai containing session data and /openai storing internal “Skills”. From ChatGPT’s self manifest, the only skill installed is a Browser.
OpenAI Agent filesystem
Beyond OpenAI, numerous teams are pushing here as well. For example, Google AI Studio (Build) can build and test apps from ideas with advanced multimodal capabilities from Gemini, and Manus orchestrates a huge number of subagents and services like browser-use to max out agent action space. I’ll leave the rest of the exploration to readers due to limited time.
Agentic RL Agent, Action (tools) and Environment (scaffold) are three tightly bound concepts in the traditional literature. Consistency between training (rollout) Gym and inference scaffold is critical to maintain agent performance. However, this consistency has become a luxury since model providers won’t expose training infra, usually resulting in tedious engineering efforts guessing the “right” scaffolding and orchestrations among downstream agent builders.
Most LLMs are trained with vanilla sandbox scaffolds to support evaluation like Terminal-Bench, yet a language model doesn’t natively “know” how to use your sandbox – especially when you want to customize the tools and “Skills”. In this case, RL becomes an effective data-efficient method for your last mile.
Rollout Infra One key challenge in Agentic RL is to build stable and efficient rollout infra. The additional factor of sandbox and tools like browser impose difficulty in asynchronous runtime efficiency, state management, and security. These rollouts are usually magnitudes more expensive (time and effort) than non-agentic RL like Math CoT. A nice starting point is OpenHands V1 released lately. The architecture of decoupled modules (abstraction, tools, sandbox, and server) provides solid coordination with reusable modules across scaffolding and rollout serving. Besides, Pytorch OpenEnv provides a nice Gymnasium-style endpoint over commonly used agent docker environments.
OpenHands V1 with decoupled modules reusable across rollout and scaffolding.
Reward Design and Training Recipe Another challenge in Agentic RL is defining the reward function. Computer-use agents usually tackle open-ended tasks like research and app building, which lack unbiased and verifiable scoring mechanisms as in Math and Coding. Meanwhile, vanilla use of LLM-as-judge to generate rewards can easily trap the policy into adversarial distribution from the teacher (Reward Hacking). This is also confirmed in Andrej’s recent interview “RL is terrible”. An interesting approach to mitigate reward hacking in open-ended research is brought by AI2 DR Tulu, where rubrics are buffered and generated on the fly together with policy. The algorithm setups used for Agentic RL generally follow the lessons from long-context RL in LLMs, such as broadening exploration in prolonged steps.
Reinforcement Learning with Evolving Rubrics (RLER)
Conclusion This post showcases the state of Computer-use Agents – from product to design principles. It elaborates the reasoning and importance of runtime sandbox in AI Agents and briefly introduces the engineering and training challenges. 2026 will be the year of Computer-use Agents – with focus shifting from Prompt Engineering to Sandbox Engineering and RL on custom scaffolds ;)
Citation Xu, Binfeng. "Demystifying Agent Sandbox". B'Log (Dec 2025). https://billxbf.github.io/posts/demystify-agent-sandbox/

content:
This is a fairly procrastinated start to my personal blog. Starting a blog isn’t as easy as it seems—I don’t want to waste people’s time with casual anecdotes. Meanwhile, an overly formal academic write-up would likely be overkill and scare people away.
There are many people who truly enjoy machine learning and find joy in sharing knowledge. I’ve been a long-time follower of AI/tech blogs from Andrej Karpathy, Lilian Weng, Yao Fu, and others. I usually prefer blogs over papers because blogs feel more honest and less AI-polished (or written to attract citations). Yet almost everyone I followed stopped posting in early 2025. I understand the shifts and hype in SF lately that keep everyone busy building and/or financially free. Still, I’d be sad if this vibe disappears—it’s been truly helpful to me over the past few years, along with many others.
I’ve been more of a builder than a writer, hesitant to publish conclusions without solid ground. But lately I’ve found it really fruitful to do deep dives into the state of things and the literature before engineering. A blog of “study notes” not only helps me organize thoughts, but also carries on that missing vibe of sharing knowledge in a blunt and unpretentious way. And so I finally made up my mind to start writing—and I truly hope you enjoy it. Stay tuned and keep the spark going!
What to Expect I’ll be writing about:
Tech Hacks: Most of the time, truth in tech world doesn’t reside in reports or posts but hidden in code. I enjoy digging these gems out to see the real flow. Different AI Research: I continuously keep track of frontier AI research and especially favor the non-incremental ones, like new architectures to model intelligence. Stories and Gossip: The true SOTA usually come from stories and gossip shared through friends, meetups, and conferences.

title: Why I start to write

content:
Binfeng Xu I’m a research engineer at NVIDIA. Currently, I work on agent harness and RL for computer-use and continual learning.
Formerly, I was a researcher at Samsung Research (SRA) where I led LLM post-training + distillation infra. I enjoy training large neural nets, building open-source projects and competing on Kaggle, where I rank top 1% globally.
Papers Gentopia: A Collaborative Platform for Tool-Augmented LLMs
Binfeng Xu, Xukun Liu, Hua Shen, Zeyu H, Yuhan L, Murong Y, Zhiyuan P, Yuchen L, Ziyu Y, Dongkuan Xu
ReWOO: Decoupling Reasoning from Observations for Efficient Augmented Language Models
Binfeng Xu, Zhiyuan Peng, Bowen Lei, Subhabrata Mukherjee, Yuchen Liu, Dongkuan Xu
Dynamic Noise Preference Optimization for LLM Self-Improvement
Haoyan Yang, Ting Hua, Shangqian Gao, Binfeng Xu, Zheng Tang, Jie Xu, Hongxia Jin, Vijay Srinivasan
Efficient Computation of Tucker Decomposition of Correlation-Based Tensors
Binfeng Xu, Grey Ballard, Robert Lyday, Paul Laurienti
Misc I play all games by Hidetaka Miyazaki, who motivated me once into indie Game Dev. Photography @Instagram; I enjoy Art. Minimalist.

Formerly, I was a researcher at Samsung Research (SRA) where I led LLM post-training + distillation infra. I enjoy training large neural nets, building open-source projects and competing on Kaggle, where I rank top 1% globally.
